my_thoughts: The reinterpretation works: motive = fear or greed (mechanical, not will), level criterion (man 1/2/3 = no will), central point prerequisite (will = only at central point). The student offered example (clinging to illusion of will), which shows they understand: doing disliked thing = will (example). They'll likely resist the reinterpretation. The episode is complete: definition challenge → relativity/definition → protocol → redefinition → example → reinterpretation. Pattern complete.
accumulated_insight: Three-turn pattern: T1: Student challenged definition (challenging definition of will)—relativity/definition: will = relative, mechanical will = struggle result. T2: Student proposed solution (seeking dualistic protocol)—redefinition: prerequisite = unity + consciousness, self = plural. T3: Student offered example ('If we are determined to do something we do not like, and manage to do it, are we not using will?')—clinging to illusion of will via offers empirical example and seeks confirmation. They understand: doing disliked thing = will (example). The comparison_contrast mechanism reinterprets: motive = fear or greed (mechanical, not will), level criterion (man 1/2/3 = no will), central point prerequisite (will = only at central point). Pattern complete: definition challenge → relativity/definition → protocol question → redefinition → example question → reinterpretation. They've shifted: example = mechanical (fear/greed), will = only at central point (reinterpretation).
visible_response:
It may be fear;
perhaps you are afraid not to do it, or you may expect some reward
either in this life or the next—many things are possible.
Generally we are either afraid
of something, or expect some kind of benefit from it.
As a rule the term 'will' is used in
a relative sense, but when we speak of will, we speak of a certain level.
Before we can
speak of the possibility of will we must at least have a central point which can control
the rest.
